junior.question: What is the primary role of residual connections in modern deep Transformer networks?
junior.preparation_guide: Explain shortcut paths, identity mappings, and gradient flow preservation.
junior.explanation: Residual connections allow activations and gradients to bypass transformer layers, preventing vanishing gradients during backpropagation and stabilizing training in deep architectures.
junior.star_framework_tip: Cite gradient flow mechanics down to early layers.
common_pitfalls: Attributing model quantization properties directly to residual connections.

junior.question: In one sentence, what does a single attention head compute?
junior.preparation_guide: State the QK-similarity weighted sum of values.
junior.explanation: A head computes attention scores from query-key similarity, softmaxes them into weights, and returns a weighted sum of the value vectors, letting each token gather information from relevant other tokens.
junior.star_framework_tip: One line: weighted sum of values by query-key similarity.
common_pitfalls: Describing attention as simple averaging without the learned QK weighting.

references.title: Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al.)

junior.question: Why do deep transformers rely on residual connections?
junior.explanation: Residual connections add each sublayer's input to its output, giving gradients a direct path through the network so very deep stacks train stably without vanishing gradients.
junior.star_framework_tip: Mention the gradient highway enabling depth.

references.title: On Layer Normalization in the Transformer Architecture

junior.question: During training, which memory consumer is usually the largest surprise beyond the weights?
junior.explanation: Optimizer states (AdamW keeps two extra tensors per parameter) and activations (scaling with batch and sequence length) usually dominate, which is why training needs far more memory than the weights alone.
junior.star_framework_tip: Name optimizer state and activations as the hidden costs.

mid_level.question: What is the difference between Pre-LN and Post-LN block designs?
mid_level.core_intent: Assesses knowledge of layer normalization placement.
mid_level.preparation_guide: Differentiate normalization position before and after sub-layers.
mid_level.explanation: Pre-LN applies Layer Normalization to the inputs of the sub-layers (attention/FFN) before adding the residual, which stabilizes training and allows training without warmup. Post-LN applies normalization after adding the residual, which can result in training instability at scale without careful initialization.
mid_level.star_framework_tip: Note how SmolLM2 and Llama use Pre-LN styles for training stability.
common_pitfalls: Claiming Pre-LN is computationally slower than Post-LN.

mid_level.question: When does Grouped-Query Attention matter most, and why?
mid_level.preparation_guide: Connect GQA to KV-cache size at long context.
mid_level.explanation: GQA matters most at long context and on memory-constrained hardware, because the KV cache grows with context length and number of KV heads; sharing KV heads shrinks that cache substantially while keeping quality close to full multi-head attention.
mid_level.star_framework_tip: Tie GQA's benefit to KV-cache pressure during long-context inference.
common_pitfalls: Thinking GQA reduces the quadratic attention compute rather than the KV cache.

references.title: GQA: Training Generalized Multi-Query Transformer Models

mid_level.question: Why does the quadratic cost of attention limit practical context length, and name one mitigation.
mid_level.preparation_guide: Recall the N x N matrix and a fix.
mid_level.explanation: Attention compares all token pairs, so memory and compute grow with the square of sequence length, which caps affordable context on small hardware. FlashAttention makes attention memory linear (via tiling and online softmax) and is a common mitigation, alongside sliding-window or sparse attention.
mid_level.star_framework_tip: State the N x N cost and one concrete mitigation.
common_pitfalls: Confusing the quadratic attention cost with linear KV-cache growth.

mid_level.question: Explain the ~16-bytes-per-parameter estimate for mixed-precision AdamW training.
mid_level.explanation: It sums 2 bytes for fp16 weights, 2 for fp16 gradients, 4 for the fp32 master weights, and 8 for the two fp32 Adam moment buffers, totaling about 16 bytes per parameter, before activations.
mid_level.star_framework_tip: Recite the byte breakdown rather than just the number.
common_pitfalls: Omitting the fp32 master copy or the optimizer moments.

senior.question: Explain memory footprint scaling differences between standard self-attention and FlashAttention during forward passes.
senior.core_intent: Tests depth in low-level memory bounds optimization and computing limits.
senior.preparation_guide: Detail HBM vs SRAM cache transfers and linear scaling of FlashAttention outputs.
senior.explanation: Standard self-attention instantiates intermediate attention score matrices of size $O(N^2)$, which are written and read from GPU High Bandwidth Memory (HBM). FlashAttention tiles inputs into SRAM blocks, performs online softmax rescales, and writes the output directly. This bypasses the $O(N^2)$ HBM transfer bottleneck, accelerating execution and keeping memory usage sub-quadratic.
senior.star_framework_tip: Detail HBM bandwidth bottlenecks vs compute-bound SRAM operations.
common_pitfalls: Stating that FlashAttention changes the mathematical output of attention (it is exact attention, not approximate).

senior.question: Why does the KV cache, not the weights, often cap long-context inference on small GPUs, and what are two mitigations?
senior.core_intent: Synthesizes attention memory and deployment limits.
senior.preparation_guide: Explain KV-cache growth and concrete fixes.
senior.explanation: At long context the KV cache grows linearly with sequence length, layers, and KV heads, and can exceed the (often quantized) weight footprint, so memory runs out even though the model itself fits. Mitigations include Grouped-Query Attention to shrink KV heads, a quantized KV cache, sliding-window attention, and PagedAttention-style allocation to reduce fragmentation.
senior.star_framework_tip: Distinguish static weight memory from growing KV memory, then give two fixes.
common_pitfalls: Assuming the advertised max context is usable regardless of device memory.

references.title: GQA: Training Generalized Multi-Query Transformer Models
Efficient Memory Management for LLM Serving (PagedAttention)

senior.question: Specify an architecture for a ~1B on-device SLM and justify each major choice.
senior.core_intent: Synthesizes the chapter into a concrete design.
senior.preparation_guide: Choose depth/width, attention, norm, activation, position, and embeddings with rationale.
senior.explanation: A ~1B on-device model would use GQA to keep the KV cache small, RMSNorm with Pre-LN for stable warmup-free training, SwiGLU for quality per parameter, RoPE for parameter-free relative positions and length flexibility, tied embeddings to reclaim the large embedding matrix, and a moderate context length matched to the device memory budget. Each choice maximizes capability per parameter and per byte.
senior.star_framework_tip: Justify every component against on-device memory and stability constraints.
common_pitfalls: Copying a large-model recipe without adapting it to the device budget.

references.title: GQA: Training Generalized Multi-Query Transformer Models
